title: Modular Variables and the Limits of Phase Detectability in Open Quantum Systems
abstract: Modular variables serve as a striking example of quantum nonlocality, particularly in superpositions of wave packets that are spatially well separated, where the relative phase between components cannot be accessed through conventional local measurements. In this work, we explore the time evolution of Hermitian modular operators for Gaussian wave-packet superpositions under the influence of a uniform gravitational field. We consider both unitary dynamics governed by the Schrödinger equation and open-system dynamics described by the Caldeira-Leggett master equation in the high-temperature limit. Adopting the Bohmian interpretation of quantum mechanics, we compute local expectation values of these modular operators along individual particle trajectories. Our analysis shows that gravitational acceleration induces a time-varying modular signal, the expectation value of the modular observable, that remains sensitive to the relative phase between the separated wave packets. In contrast, standard local quantities such as the probability density and probability current, while modified by gravity, become insensitive to the relative phase in the regime of negligible spatial overlap. For a pair of particles coupled to a shared environment, we find that environment-induced correlations can modify the local modular expectation value observed for one particle, yielding a clear signature of environmental influence. However, the transfer of phase sensitivity via environment-generated entanglement to the modular signal of the distant particle remains negligible within the regime considered. We further demonstrate that conventional measures of coherence and entanglement do not capture the relative phase information in this non-overlapping regime.
summary: This paper demonstrates that modular variables can act as sensitive probes for nonlocal phase information in quantum superpositions, even when wave packets are spatially separated and subject to environmental decoherence. While thermal noise reduces the signal amplitude, the phase information remains accessible, unlike with standard local observables. The study highlights the utility of modular operators in detecting quantum features in the presence of dissipation and gravity.
main_problem: Investigating whether relative phase information in spatially non-overlapping quantum superpositions can be detected using modular variables in the presence of environmental noise and gravity.
main_result: Modular variables remain sensitive to the relative phase even when wave packets do not overlap, whereas standard local quantities and information-theoretic measures like entanglement fail to capture this phase. Thermal noise damps the signal amplitude, but the phase-sensitive structure remains robust.
method: The study uses the Bohmian mechanics framework to compute local expectation values along particle trajectories and employs the Caldeira-Leggett master equation to model open-system dynamics.
model_or_system: Gaussian wave-packet superpositions (single and two-particle systems) subject to a uniform gravitational field and coupled to a common environment modeled by the Caldeira-Leggett equation in the high-temperature limit.
key_observables: Modular operators (modular momentum and translation operators), probability density, probability current, and information-theoretic measures (coherence, entropy, and entanglement).
important_parameters: Spatial separation (L), gravitational acceleration (g), relaxation rate (gamma), temperature (T), and quantum statistics (Bose-Einstein, Fermi-Dirac, and Maxwell-Boltzmann).
main_assumptions: High-temperature limit, Born-Markov approximation, Ohmic spectral density, and the regime of negligible spatial overlap between wave packets.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares density plots of unitary vs. dissipative dynamics; Figure 2 shows the time evolution of local modular expectation values; Figure 3 demonstrates phase dependence for different relative phases; Figure 4 shows the impact of temperature and relaxation on two-particle modular signals.
paper_structure: The paper derives the equation of motion for the displacement operator, analyzes Gaussian wave-packet superpositions under unitary and dissipative dynamics, extends the analysis to multi-particle systems with different quantum statistics, and concludes by comparing modular variables to standard local observables.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to access nonlocal phase information in open quantum systems where standard decoherence-related metrics like entanglement or coherence fail, offering a potential probe for gravity-induced effects in the presence of noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Density plots of the probability density for the Schr¨odinger dynamics (left) and the CL dynamics (right) at γ = 0.1 and T = 10. A representative set of Bohmian trajectories is superimposed. These trajectories follow the motion of the left wave packet, while the right wave packet remains empty. The middle blue trajectory corresponds to the wave-packet center. The remaining parameters are fixed as ¯h = m = kB = σ0 = 1, k = 0.1, L = 50, and g = −3.

FIG. 2: Local expectation value of the modular variable along Bohmian trajectories for a relative phase α = π/4 and three different Bohmian initial positions: X0 = −L/2 −2σ0 (brown), X0 = −L/2 (magenta), and X0 = −L/2 + 2σ0 (cyan). The left panel shows the Schr¨odinger dynamics [Eq. (34)], and the right panel shows the CL dynamics. The remaining parameters are ¯h = m = kB = σ0 = 1, k = 0.1, L = 50, g = −3, γ = 0.001, and T = 2.

FIG. 3: Local expectation value of modular variable along the Bohmian trajectory with initial position X0 = −L/2, shown for different values of the relative phase α: α = 0 (top left), α = π/4 (top right), α = π/2 (bottom left), and α = π (bottom right). In each panel, the black curve corresponds to the Schr¨odinger dynamics [Eq. (34)], while the red and green curves correspond to the CL framework with temperatures T = 2 and T = 5, respectively, and a relaxation rate γ = 0.001. The remaining parameters are ¯h = m = kB = σ0 = 1, k = 0.1, L = 50, and g = −3.

FIG. 4: Expectation value of the modular variable of the first particle [Eq. (59)] for relative phases α = 0 (left panel) and α = π/2 (right panel). Colored curves correspond to the CL framework with relaxation rate γ = 0.005 and temperatures T = 2 (red), T = 5 (green), and T = 15 (blue). Other parameters are ¯h = m = kB = σ0 = 1, k = 0.1, g = −3 and L = 50.
